Just now

--

How a unit-pricing bug turned $0.19 accounts into trillion-dollar debtors, and why a single assert would have stopped it.
If you run anything on AWS, your cost alarms are wired into your nervous system. A budget alert pages finance. A spend anomaly pages security. That wiring exists because you trust the number on the dashboard. On July 17, 2026, that trust got a stress test nobody scheduled.
A UK charity that pays under a pound a month opened its console to a projected charge of £5.8 billion, the account owner told The Guardian he nearly had a heart attack. The guy behind CollegeFootballData.com, whose bill hadn’t cleared two cents in six years, woke up to an alert claiming $1.5 billion in usage with $3 billion projected. A Reddit user with $0.19 in charges got an estimate of nearly $2.5 billion. On Hacker News, one account normally billing about a dollar showed $1.7 billion. One widely shared screenshot documented a month-over-month change of 55 trillion percent.
Not one of these numbers was real. But every alarm they triggered was.
